posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
• 2018 Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée (7/28/2021)
    Wednesday night after a trying day at work, and I needed something good. This was at hand. Two hours to open up (working from home means you can let a bottle air while still on Zoom calls) then it was down to business. Deep blackberry, Darjeeling tea, spice, cedar and moist earth all come through on the nose. It’s a little bit aggressive on the palate, so time is definitely your friend. I will wait on my other bottles, and definitely wait on the Colombiere. Of course I say that now, and will most likely have a loss of willpower sooner rather than later.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: One of the best village vosnes out there. Worth the coin to me. I don’t get offers any more.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
One of the best village vosnes out there. Worth the coin to me. I don’t get offers any more.

I used to get a six pack, and maybe an extra bottle. Last year it was 2 in total. I don’t like buying singletons of stuff like this, so 2018 was likely my swan song. Still, it was a great ride while it lasted, and I have a decent stash to take me through.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
Completely beyond the bounds of reason.

We are talking about Burgundy. Check out any Cecile Tremblay wines from a recent vintage. It seems that being female, at least in Burgundy, does not signify a lack of respect for your skills. Seems the female owners and winemakers are getting chased more intensely than equivalent domaines owned by gents. I have been telling my daughters that in 10 years women are going to be running everything. Hopefully I can retire at that point and just relax!

Viva Egalite!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Completely beyond the bounds of reason.

We are talking about Burgundy. Check out any Cecile Tremblay wines from a recent vintage. It seems that being female, at least in Burgundy, does not signify a lack of respect for your skills.

As someone who buys both Tremblay and M-G, I think it has a lot more to do with their wines being great than the fact that they’re women. There are certainly female winemakers in Burgundy who don’t command a premium; Taupenot-Merme, Tortochot, etc.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
Completely beyond the bounds of reason.

We are talking about Burgundy. Check out any Cecile Tremblay wines from a recent vintage. It seems that being female, at least in Burgundy, does not signify a lack of respect for your skills.

As someone who buys both Tremblay and M-G, I think it has a lot more to do with their wines being great than the fact that they’re women. There are certainly female winemakers in Burgundy who don’t command a premium; Taupenot-Merme, Tortochot, etc.

Point is, being female does not hurt their image. They are being judged on their skills. Taupenot-Merme probably does deserve more respect. Good wines. You are indeed a lucky, (and probably wealthy, well connected guy!) to be able to drink Tremblay regularly. Have not had any experience with her wines but based on critical reviews, it appears the price is probably more based on a cultish identity and rarity rather than purely what is in the bottle. But I will defer to your greater experience. What do you think makes Tremblay so desirable?

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked: For C Tremblay, if you can’t get them en primeur, the only option is to drink them in restaurants where they are priced based on property door price (times 3 or 4). To buy them on the secondary market is crazy. The few (bourgogne, village and 1er cru) I have had have been quite enjoyable.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Westgarth always overprices allocated wines looking for the biggest sucker. Can’t use their prices as market.

They are the cheapest in this case.

Don’t get me wrong - they often show up as the cheapest available on WS. But that’s because it’s on highly allocated wines that never see the light of day at reasonable prices. Doesn’t make them the market price. They typically are way over even auction prices.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: Westgarth rarely even has the actual wine in stock. So using them as a price indicator of anything is worthless.
